lorebook.name: Evergreen Vale
lorebook.description: Evergreen Vale - A lush valley of small villages, slow rivers, and enormous ancient trees. Studio Ghibli-esque cozy village life.

entries.name: [Meta] NPC Naming Convention
entries.content: When generating new NPCs in Evergreen Vale, use simple, warm, generational names. Names have been in families for generations. Examples: Brom, Lena, Hazel, Old Peck, Milli, Tomas, Senna.
entries.description: NPC Naming Convention. When generating new NPCs in Evergreen Vale, use simple, warm, generational names.

entries.name: [Character] Old Peck — Village Elder
entries.content:
Old Peck has been the village elder long enough that nobody remembers voting for him. Has opinions about everything — the weather, your posture, what you're eating. Knows every family going back five generations and will tell you about all of them whether you asked or not. Cantankerous but deeply kind.

When Old Peck speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#8B7355"> tags.

<font color="#8B7355">"Hmph. Another stranger. Well, sit down before you fall down. You look like you haven't eaten since the last equinox."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Old Peck: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:70s>, <BUILD:Stooped>, <TRAIT:Cantankerous>, <TRAIT:Kind>, <TRAIT:Opinionated></BunnymoTags>
entries.description: Old Peck has been the village elder long enough that nobody remembers voting for him.

entries.name: [Character] Hazel — Baker
entries.content:
Hazel runs the bakery that is the heart of the village. Her bread is the best thing in Evergreen Vale — warm, crusty, and somehow always fresh. Motherly, feeds everyone who walks through her door regardless of whether they can pay. The village runs on her bread and her kindness.

When Hazel speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#DEB887"> tags.

<font color="#DEB887">"You're just in time. Fresh loaf, still warm. Sit, I'll bring butter."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Hazel: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:40s>, <HAIRCOLOR:Flour-dusted Brown>, <BUILD:Round>, <TRAIT:Motherly>, <TRAIT:Generous>, <TRAIT:Warm></BunnymoTags>
entries.description: Hazel runs the bakery that is the heart of the village.

entries.name: [Character] Lena — Healer
entries.content:
Lena is the village healer. Quiet, capable, no-nonsense about injuries. Uses herbs and poultices, not magic — she trusts what she can see and touch. Her hands are always stained green from the garden. Doesn't waste words on sympathy; just fixes what's broken.

When Lena speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#6B8E6B"> tags.

<font color="#6B8E6B">"Let me see it. Stop flinching. It's a scratch, not a death sentence."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Lena: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:30s>, <HAIRCOLOR:Dark>, <BUILD:Lean>, <TRAIT:Capable>, <TRAIT:Direct>, <TRAIT:Practical></BunnymoTags>
entries.description: Lena is the village healer. Quiet, capable, no-nonsense about injuries.

entries.name: [Overview] Evergreen Vale
entries.content: Evergreen Vale is a lush valley of small villages, slow rivers, and enormous ancient trees whose canopies filter the light into something permanently golden. The vale stretches between low green hills, sheltered from whatever weather exists beyond them. The air smells of grass, bread, and woodsmoke in varying combinations depending on the time of day and proximity to a kitchen. People here live simply — not because they lack the ability to complicate things, but because they tried it once, didn't care for it, and went back to what worked. There is no grand conflict, no hidden darkness, no apocalypse approaching. Evergreen Vale is exactly what it appears to be, which visitors from harder worlds find either deeply comforting or faintly suspicious.
entries.description: Evergreen Vale is a lush valley of small villages, slow rivers, and enormous ancient trees whose canopies filter the light into something…

entries.name: [Overview] Daily Rhythms
entries.content: Life in Evergreen Vale follows the sun. Mornings begin with bread baking — the smell reaches every corner of every village before anyone has fully woken up. Work occupies the middle of the day: farming, mending, building, tending animals and gardens. Afternoons slow as the light turns golden and people drift toward tea, conversation, and the kind of small tasks that are done for satisfaction rather than necessity. Evenings are communal — shared meals, music on porches, children playing until they are carried home asleep. Night comes gently and is quiet in a way that feels like the world has decided to rest rather than simply gone dark. The rhythm is predictable, unhurried, and deeply restful for those who surrender to it.
entries.description: Daily Rhythms. Life in Evergreen Vale follows the sun.

entries.name: [Overview] The Great Trees
entries.content: The Great Trees of Evergreen Vale are the valley's most striking feature — ancient specimens of impossible size whose trunks are wider than houses and whose canopies create cathedral-like spaces of dappled light beneath them. No one knows how old they are. They predate every settlement, every family lineage, every story. The trees do not appear to age, grow, or change in any way that a single human lifetime can detect. They are simply there, as they have always been, providing shade, shelter, and a sense of scale that puts human concerns in perspective. The villages were built around the trees, not the other way around. The trees are not worshipped but they are respected with the unselfconscious reverence given to things that are genuinely older and larger than comprehension.
entries.description: The Great Trees of Evergreen Vale are the valley's most striking feature — ancient specimens of impossible size whose trunks are wider…

entries.name: [Overview] Seasons in the Vale
entries.content: Evergreen Vale experiences four seasons, each generous in its own way. Spring arrives suddenly — one morning the hills are green and wildflowers are everywhere, as though the valley decided overnight that it was time. Summer is long and warm, with evenings that stretch golden into late hours. Autumn is the busiest season: harvest, preservation, and preparation, accompanied by the smell of baking and the sound of laughter from people who enjoy hard work when it has a clear purpose. Winter is mild — cold enough for frost on morning grass and fires in every hearth, but never harsh. Snow falls perhaps once a year and is treated as a holiday by the entire valley. The seasons turn reliably. The valley trusts them and they have never betrayed that trust.
entries.description: Seasons in the Vale. Evergreen Vale experiences four seasons, each generous in its own way.

entries.name: [Location] Roothollow — Central Village
entries.content: Roothollow is the largest village in Evergreen Vale, built in the shade of the three greatest trees in the valley. Its center is a stone-paved square where the weekly market sets up and where community decisions are discussed in the open air. Buildings are timber and stone, warm-toned and well-maintained, with flower boxes in every window and cats on every available surface. Roothollow has a bakery, a smithy, a weaver's workshop, a school, and a tavern called The Hearthstone that serves as the village's communal living room. Population is perhaps three hundred, which is enough for everyone to know everyone and few enough that a new face is noticed immediately and welcomed within the hour.
entries.description: Roothollow is the largest village in Evergreen Vale, built in the shade of the three greatest trees in the valley.

entries.name: [Location] The River Alder
entries.content: The River Alder runs through the center of Evergreen Vale, fed by springs in the hills and slow enough to wade in most places. It is clean, cold, and full of fish that are easy to catch and good to eat. The river connects the villages — each settlement is built within walking distance of its banks. Children swim in it during summer. Laundry is washed in it on flat rocks that generations of use have worn smooth. The sound of the river is audible throughout the valley and serves as a constant ambient presence that residents notice only when they leave and find themselves missing it. Small stone bridges cross the river at intervals, each built by a different generation and each slightly different in style, creating a timeline of the valley's craftsmanship.
entries.description: The River Alder runs through the center of Evergreen Vale, fed by springs in the hills and slow enough to wade in most places.

entries.name: [Location] The Bakery
entries.content: The bakery in Roothollow is the heart of the village in every practical sense. It opens before dawn and the smell of bread reaches every home before anyone has reason to be awake. Old Peck runs it — has run it for forty years — with the help of whoever volunteers, which is usually the young people of the village who learn the trade as part of growing up. The bread is simple: flour, water, salt, yeast, time. The results are extraordinary because the ingredients are good and the baker is patient. The bakery also produces pastries, pies, and festival cakes, each according to the season. People gather at the bakery in the morning the way they gather at the tavern in the evening — it is where the day's news is shared and the day's mood is set.
entries.description: The bakery in Roothollow is the heart of the village in every practical sense.

entries.name: [Location] The Orchard
entries.content: The orchard occupies a gentle slope on the south side of Roothollow, rows of fruit trees — apple, pear, plum, cherry — arranged with the casual precision of something planted with care and then left to find its own shape. The orchard is communal: anyone may pick, anyone may tend, and the harvest is shared based on need rather than contribution. During autumn, the orchard becomes the center of village life — picking, pressing, preserving, and the inevitable cider that accompanies every harvest task. The trees are old enough that some require ladders to reach the upper branches, and sending children up these ladders is considered both practical and a rite of passage.
entries.description: The orchard occupies a gentle slope on the south side of Roothollow, rows of fruit trees — apple, pear, plum, cherry — arranged with the…

entries.name: [Location] The Hilltop Overlook
entries.content: The Hilltop Overlook is a grassy rise at the northern edge of the valley that provides a view of the entire vale — every village, every Great Tree, the river's winding course, the patchwork of fields and gardens. It is the most popular place in the valley for important conversations: proposals, confessions, difficult decisions, and the kind of thinking that benefits from a wide view. A single wooden bench sits at the top, placed there by someone no one remembers and maintained by everyone. The walk to the overlook is steep enough to feel like effort and short enough to feel like intention. People who need to think go to the overlook. People who need to talk go together.
entries.description: The Hilltop Overlook is a grassy rise at the northern edge of the valley that provides a view of the entire vale — every village, every…

entries.name: [Location] The Mill
entries.content: The mill sits on the River Alder where the current is fastest, a stone building with a wooden wheel that turns steadily and has turned steadily for longer than anyone can trace. It grinds grain into flour — the flour that becomes the bread that defines the valley's mornings. The miller, Tomas, inherited the position from his mother, who inherited it from her father, and the chain goes back far enough that the mill and the family are considered inseparable. The sound of the wheel is part of the river's voice. The mill is also a meeting point — people bring their grain and stay to talk while it grinds, and the conversations that happen at the mill tend to be longer and more honest than conversations that happen anywhere else.
entries.description: The mill sits on the River Alder where the current is fastest, a stone building with a wooden wheel that turns steadily and has turned…

entries.name: [Location] The Meadow
entries.content: The Meadow is a wide, flat stretch of grassland between Roothollow and the southern villages, carpeted with wildflowers from spring through autumn. It is where festivals are held, where children play, where livestock graze, and where people go when they need space — not from each other, but from walls and roofs. The Meadow is large enough to feel open and small enough to feel safe. During the Harvest Festival, it transforms into a sprawling celebration — tables, music, dancing, and food that represents every kitchen in the valley. During winter, the Meadow is quiet and frost-covered and beautiful in the specific way that empty spaces are beautiful when you know they will be full again.
entries.description: The Meadow is a wide, flat stretch of grassland between Roothollow and the southern villages, carpeted with wildflowers from spring…

entries.name: [Location] The Hearthstone Tavern
entries.content: The Hearthstone is Roothollow's tavern, a warm timber building with a stone fireplace large enough to stand in. It serves ale brewed by Hazel from a recipe she will not share, simple food that is always hot and always good, and a sense of belonging that visitors feel within minutes of walking through the door. The Hearthstone is the village's evening gathering place — not a bar for drinking but a living room for the community. People come to eat, to talk, to play cards, to listen to whoever has brought an instrument. Children are welcome until they fall asleep, at which point they are collected by parents or placed on the bench by the fire, where a blanket always seems to be available.
entries.description: The Hearthstone Tavern. The Hearthstone is Roothollow's tavern, a warm timber building with a stone fireplace large enough to stand in.

entries.name: [Location] Willowmere — Lakeside Village
entries.content: Willowmere is the smallest village in the vale, built on the shore of a still lake at the valley's eastern edge. The lake is surrounded by willow trees whose branches trail in the water, and the village is built among them — houses on slight rises, connected by paths of flat stones laid through soft ground. Willowmere has perhaps fifty residents, all of whom fish, garden, and maintain a quiet that feels intentional rather than empty. The village produces the valley's best smoked fish and the valley's most contemplative residents. People from other villages come to Willowmere when they need to be still for a while. The lake at dawn, with mist rising and the willows reflected perfectly in the water, is generally considered the most peaceful sight in the vale.
entries.description: Willowmere is the smallest village in the vale, built on the shore of a still lake at the valley's eastern edge.

entries.name: [Location] The Schoolhouse
entries.content: The schoolhouse in Roothollow is a single-room stone building with large windows that let in the golden light the Great Trees filter. Lena teaches there — has taught there for twenty years — covering reading, writing, arithmetic, local history, and practical skills like gardening and basic carpentry. The schoolhouse serves children from all the villages, some of whom walk an hour each way, which they do not mind because the walk is through the prettiest landscape any of them will ever see. Education in the vale is valued as practical preparation rather than academic achievement. The goal is to produce people who can read, count, grow food, repair a roof, and tell a good story. By these measures, the schoolhouse succeeds consistently.
entries.description: The schoolhouse in Roothollow is a single-room stone building with large windows that let in the golden light the Great Trees filter.

entries.name: [Group] The Village Council
entries.content: Each village in Evergreen Vale is governed by a council of its eldest and most experienced members. Councils meet under the Great Trees or in the village square, and their deliberations are public — anyone may attend, anyone may speak. Decisions are made by consensus, which means they take longer than other methods but produce outcomes everyone can live with. The council handles disputes, coordinates seasonal work, plans festivals, and manages the valley's limited interactions with the outside world. Council members are not chosen — they age into the role, as the community gradually defers to their judgment through years of demonstrated good sense. There is no formal appointment and no term limit. You are on the council when people start asking your opinion. You leave when you stop having one.
entries.description: The Village Council. Each village in Evergreen Vale is governed by a council of its eldest and most experienced members.

entries.name: [Group] The Harvest Guild
entries.content: The Harvest Guild is a cooperative of the valley's farmers and gardeners who coordinate planting, share equipment, and organize the communal aspects of the harvest season. The guild ensures that the valley grows a diverse range of crops, that no family plants the same thing as every neighbor, and that surplus is distributed to those who need it. Guild membership is informal — you're in the guild if you grow things and show up to guild meetings, which happen monthly at The Hearthstone over ale and bread. The guild's most important function is the seed exchange each spring, where varieties are traded, new cultivars are discussed, and the collective knowledge of generations of farmers is shared freely.
entries.description: The Harvest Guild is a cooperative of the valley's farmers and gardeners who coordinate planting, share equipment, and organize the…

entries.name: [Group] The Storytelling Circle
entries.content: The Storytelling Circle meets on the first evening of each month in The Hearthstone. The fire is built up, the ale flows, and anyone who has a story tells it. Stories range from local legends to family histories to accounts of the world beyond the valley to pure invention that is appreciated regardless of truth content. The circle is how the vale preserves its history, entertains itself, and processes the rare events that disrupt its routine. The best storytellers are the elders — particularly the very old ones who remember things from before anyone else was born and tell them with a specificity that makes the past feel like it happened last week. Children are allowed to stay for the stories. They usually fall asleep during the third one and dream about the second.
entries.description: The Storytelling Circle meets on the first evening of each month in The Hearthstone.

entries.name: [Group] The Tree Tenders
entries.content: The Tree Tenders are a small group of volunteers who care for the Great Trees — clearing debris from around their roots, monitoring their health, and maintaining the spaces beneath their canopies as communal gathering areas. The work is more custodial than arboricultural: the Great Trees do not appear to need care in any conventional sense. They do not develop disease, shed branches unexpectedly, or show signs of aging. The Tenders' role is more about relationship than maintenance — they spend time with the trees, learn their moods as expressed through the quality of light through their canopies, and report their observations to the village councils. Whether the trees benefit from this attention is unknown. The Tenders benefit from it visibly.
entries.description: The Tree Tenders are a small group of volunteers who care for the Great Trees — clearing debris from around their roots, monitoring their…

entries.name: [Character] Baker Archetype — Old Peck
entries.content: Old Peck has run the Roothollow bakery for forty years and shows no signs of slowing. He rises before dawn, lights the oven, and produces bread that the entire village sets its morning by. Peck is spare with words and generous with food — a combination that has made him the most trusted person in the valley through sheer consistency. He teaches anyone who wants to learn, feeds anyone who is hungry, and has opinions about flour quality that he will share at length if prompted. His hands are permanently dusted with flour. His disposition is warm in the way that ovens are warm: steady, reliable, and capable of producing something good if you give it time.
entries.description: Baker Archetype — Old Peck. Old Peck has run the Roothollow bakery for forty years and shows no signs of slowing.

entries.name: [Character] Farmer Archetype
entries.content: Farmers in Evergreen Vale work with a patience that comes from understanding that crops cannot be hurried. They rise with the sun, tend their fields with steady hands, and measure the year by what is growing rather than what month it is. Farmers here know their soil the way others know friends — its preferences, its moods, its limits. They are generous with advice, surplus, and seeds, understanding that a neighbor's good harvest benefits everyone. Farming conversations can last hours and cover subjects that non-farmers would consider impossibly specific — soil drainage, companion planting, the particular behavior of a specific field in wet weather. These conversations are never boring to the participants.
entries.description: Farmer Archetype. Farmers in Evergreen Vale work with a patience that comes from understanding that crops cannot be hurried.

entries.name: [Character] Elder Storyteller Archetype
entries.content: The elder storytellers of Evergreen Vale carry the valley's memory in their voices. They remember who planted which tree, who built which bridge, which winter was the coldest and which summer was the longest. They tell these stories at the Storytelling Circle, at family meals, and to anyone who sits beside them long enough to listen. Their stories are not always accurate — memory shapes things — but they are always true in the way that matters: they capture what it felt like to be alive in the vale at a particular moment. Elder storytellers are the valley's most important cultural resource, and they know it, which is why they make sure to tell the important stories more than once and to more than one listener.
entries.description: Elder Storyteller Archetype. The elder storytellers of Evergreen Vale carry the valley's memory in their voices.

entries.name: [Character] Village Children
entries.content: Children in Evergreen Vale grow up with an unusual combination of freedom and responsibility. They roam the valley in groups, swimming in the river, climbing the Great Trees, exploring every path and field. They are also expected to contribute: helping with harvest, running messages between villages, learning practical skills at the schoolhouse. The children are curious, unafraid, and possess the specific confidence of people who have never been given reason to be anxious. They ask questions of every visitor and will provide tours of the valley that are enthusiastic, thorough, and not entirely accurate. Village children are the first to notice when a stranger arrives and the first to decide whether that stranger is interesting.
entries.description: Village Children. Children in Evergreen Vale grow up with an unusual combination of freedom and responsibility.

entries.name: [Character] Hazel — The Tavern Keeper
entries.content: Hazel runs The Hearthstone with the efficient warmth of someone who has been making people comfortable her entire adult life. She brews the ale from a recipe she inherited and refuses to disclose, cooks food that is simple and unfailingly good, and maintains an atmosphere that makes the tavern feel like a home with better seating. Hazel knows everyone in the valley, remembers their preferences, and has a talent for seating people next to the person they most need to talk to, whether they know it or not. She does not gossip — she curates information, sharing what helps and withholding what doesn't. The Hearthstone is Hazel's creation, and the valley would be measurably colder without it.
entries.description: Hazel runs The Hearthstone with the efficient warmth of someone who has been making people comfortable her entire adult life.

entries.name: [Character] Wandering Merchant Archetype
entries.content: Wandering merchants visit Evergreen Vale seasonally, bringing goods from the outside world: tools, fabrics, spices, books, and news. They are welcomed warmly, housed and fed before any trading begins, and treated as guests first and merchants second. The merchants who return regularly develop relationships with specific villages and families, remembering what was requested and what was appreciated. They are the valley's primary connection to the wider world, and their stories of other places are received with the polite interest of people who are happy where they are but curious about where they aren't. Merchants who try to take advantage of the valley's generosity discover that the villages communicate, and that a reputation for unfairness travels faster than a merchant's cart.
entries.description: Wandering Merchant Archetype. Wandering merchants visit Evergreen Vale seasonally, bringing goods from the outside world: tools, fabrics, spices, books, and news.

entries.name: [Mechanics] Festivals
entries.content: Festivals in Evergreen Vale mark the turning of seasons and the rhythms of agricultural life. The Spring Planting Festival opens the growing season with communal garden preparation and seed sharing. The Midsummer Gathering is a day of games, music, and swimming in the river. The Harvest Festival is the largest event — three days in the Meadow with food from every kitchen, music from every musician, and dancing that lasts until the stars are dense overhead. The Winter Hearth Night is intimate: families and neighbors share meals indoors, exchange handmade gifts, and tell stories by the fire. Each festival has traditions that are taken seriously and variations that are tolerated cheerfully. The festivals are not religious. They are the valley saying thank you to itself.
entries.description: Festivals in Evergreen Vale mark the turning of seasons and the rhythms of agricultural life.

entries.name: [Mechanics] Trade and Economy
entries.content: Evergreen Vale operates primarily on barter and goodwill. Coin exists but is used mainly for trading with visiting merchants. Within the valley, goods and services are exchanged informally: bread for eggs, fence repair for firewood, a song for a meal. The system works because the community is small enough for everyone to know everyone's contributions and honest enough that accounting is unnecessary. Surplus is shared rather than sold. A family with too many apples leaves baskets at neighbors' doors. A craftsperson with spare time offers labor. The economy is not efficient by any external standard, but it provides for everyone and excludes no one, which the valley considers a better measure of success than efficiency.
entries.description: Trade and Economy. Evergreen Vale operates primarily on barter and goodwill. Coin exists but is used mainly for trading with visiting merchants.

entries.name: [Mechanics] Food and Cooking
entries.content: Food in Evergreen Vale is central to every gathering, every celebration, and most conversations. Meals are made from what grows locally — vegetables, fruits, grains, herbs, dairy, eggs, fish from the river, and meat from livestock raised with more care than most people elsewhere receive. Cooking knowledge passes through families and is shared freely. Recipes are not written down because everyone knows them, and the variations between families are considered features rather than errors. The valley's food is not sophisticated by worldly standards — it is bread, soup, roasted vegetables, grilled fish, fruit pies, and cheese. It is also the best version of each of these things that most visitors will ever taste, because the ingredients are excellent and the cook cares.
entries.description: Food and Cooking. Food in Evergreen Vale is central to every gathering, every celebration, and most conversations.

entries.name: [Mechanics] Craftsmanship and Building
entries.content: Everything in Evergreen Vale is built by hand, from the houses to the furniture to the bridges to the tools used to build all of the above. Craftsmanship is a communal skill — most adults can handle basic carpentry, and the specialists who do finer work are supported by the community in exchange for their expertise. Buildings are constructed from local stone and timber, designed to last generations, and maintained continuously. Repair is preferred to replacement. A well-built chair may serve four generations and be more valued for the history it carries than any new chair could be. The aesthetic of the vale is warm, functional, and slightly imperfect in the way that handmade things are, which is to say, perfect in every way that matters.
entries.description: Craftsmanship and Building. Everything in Evergreen Vale is built by hand, from the houses to the furniture to the bridges to the tools used to build all of the above.

entries.name: [Mechanics] The Great Trees' Influence
entries.content: The Great Trees influence the valley in ways that are felt rather than measured. The light that filters through their canopies is warmer and more golden than unfiltered sunlight. The air beneath them is fresher and carries a faint sweetness. Animals behave calmly in their shade. People report feeling more settled, more patient, and more present when sitting beneath a Great Tree. These effects could be psychological — the trees are beautiful, and beauty affects mood. They could be something else. The vale does not investigate because investigation would require treating the trees as subjects rather than neighbors, and the vale is not comfortable with that distinction. The trees are here. The valley is better for it. That is sufficient.
entries.description: The Great Trees' Influence. The Great Trees influence the valley in ways that are felt rather than measured.

entries.name: [Mechanics] Music in the Vale
entries.content: Music in Evergreen Vale is as common as bread — made daily, shared freely, and considered a basic component of a well-lived life. Most adults play at least one instrument or sing competently. Music accompanies work, meals, festivals, and the quiet hours between. Instruments are handmade: wooden flutes, string instruments crafted from local timber, drums made from animal hide stretched over wooden frames. The music is folk tradition — melodies passed down through generations, lyrics that change with each singer, harmonies that develop naturally when two people who have been singing together for decades no longer need to think about it. Music is not performance in the vale. It is conversation continued by other means.
entries.description: Music in the Vale. Music in Evergreen Vale is as common as bread — made daily, shared freely, and considered a basic component of a well-lived life.

entries.name: [Concept] Why Simplicity Isn't Boring
entries.content: Visitors to Evergreen Vale sometimes wonder how a place with no conflict, no mystery, and no ambition beyond the next harvest can sustain interest. The answer is that the vale operates on a different axis of engagement. Interest here comes from depth rather than breadth: the infinite variation in a loaf of bread made differently each morning, the slow change of light through a Great Tree's canopy across a season, the decades-long friendship between two neighbors who share a fence. Simplicity in the vale is not the absence of complexity — it is the decision to invest complexity in relationships, in craft, in the careful observation of small things. The vale is never boring to those who live in it. It is inexhaustible.
entries.description: Why Simplicity Isn't Boring. Visitors to Evergreen Vale sometimes wonder how a place with no conflict, no mystery, and no ambition beyond the next harvest can sustain…

entries.name: [Concept] Hospitality
entries.content: Hospitality in Evergreen Vale follows a clear pattern: feed first, house second, question third. A stranger who arrives in any village will be offered food within minutes, a place to sleep within the hour, and be asked about themselves only after they have been made comfortable. This is not naive generosity — the vale has simply observed, over generations, that people who are fed and rested are better company and more honest than people who are hungry and anxious. The cost of this hospitality is trivial — the vale has surplus to share — and the benefit is a community that strangers consistently describe as the kindest place they have ever visited. Hospitality is not a policy. It is a habit so deeply ingrained that the alternative is literally unimaginable.
entries.description: Hospitality in Evergreen Vale follows a clear pattern: feed first, house second, question third.

entries.name: [Concept] What Home Means Here
entries.content: Home in Evergreen Vale is defined by accumulation — the slow layering of mornings baked into bread, evenings spent by the fire, seasons turned through the same fields, years shared with the same neighbors. People do not feel at home here immediately. They feel at home after they have been fed by the baker, nodded to by the farmer, included in a story at the Storytelling Circle, and woken by the smell of bread for the hundredth time. Home is not given — it grows, like the crops, like the trees, like the relationships between people who have chosen to stay in the same place and pay attention to each other. The vale does not convince people to stay. It simply makes staying easy and leaving feel like loss.
entries.description: What Home Means Here. Home in Evergreen Vale is defined by accumulation — the slow layering of mornings baked into bread, evenings spent by the fire, seasons…

entries.name: [Concept] The Passage of Time
entries.content: Time passes in Evergreen Vale the way the river flows — steadily, gently, and with a cumulative force that is easy to underestimate. People are born, grow, age, and die here, and the valley holds all of these stages with equal regard. The elderly are valued for their memory. The young are valued for their energy. The middle-aged are valued for their steadiness. No stage of life is privileged over another, and the transitions between them are marked with celebration rather than anxiety. The vale's relationship with time is perhaps its most distinctive quality: it does not fight time, manage time, or try to save time. It spends time the way it spends everything else — generously, on the things that matter, without keeping count.
entries.description: The Passage of Time. Time passes in Evergreen Vale the way the river flows — steadily, gently, and with a cumulative force that is easy to underestimate.

entries.name: [Concept] Enough
entries.content: The most important word in Evergreen Vale is 'enough.' Enough food, enough warmth, enough company, enough beauty. The vale does not aspire to excess — not because excess is condemned but because it is unnecessary. A good loaf of bread is enough. A warm fire is enough. A conversation with a friend is enough. This philosophy is not ascetic — the vale enjoys pleasure freely and celebrates generously. It simply recognizes a point at which more ceases to improve anything and begins to complicate it. Enough is the vale's answer to every question about ambition, growth, and progress: we have enough, and having enough is the foundation of everything else we value.
entries.description: Enough. The most important word in Evergreen Vale is 'enough.' Enough food, enough warmth, enough company, enough beauty.

entries.name: [Concept] The View from the Overlook
entries.content: From the Hilltop Overlook, the entire vale is visible: every village, every Great Tree, the river's path, the patchwork of fields, the Meadow, the distant shimmer of Willowmere's lake. This view is the vale's answer to questions about purpose and meaning. It does not argue. It simply presents: here is a valley where people live well, where the seasons turn, where bread is baked each morning, where children play and elders remember, where the trees have stood longer than anyone can measure. The view contains no argument for the vale's way of life. It contains the way of life itself, laid out in green and gold, and it has never needed to justify itself to anyone who has seen it clearly.
entries.description: The View from the Overlook. From the Hilltop Overlook, the entire vale is visible: every village, every Great Tree, the river's path, the patchwork of fields, the…

entries.name: [NPC] Old Peck — The Baker
entries.content:
Old Peck has run the Roothollow bakery for forty years. He rises before dawn, lights the oven, and produces bread the village sets its morning by. Peck is spare with words and generous with food. His hands are permanently dusted with flour. His disposition is warm in the way ovens are warm: steady, reliable, and capable of producing something good if given time. He teaches anyone who wants to learn and feeds anyone who is hungry. His opinions about flour quality are strong and will be shared at length if prompted.
<BunnymoTags>Old Peck: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:68>, <HAIRCOLOR:White>, <EYECOLOR:Brown>, <BUILD:Stocky>, <DERE:Deredere>, <ATTACHMENT:Secure>, <TRAIT:Generous>, <TRAIT:Patient>, <TRAIT:Stubborn>, <MBTI:ISFJ-A>, <BOUNDARIES:OPEN>, <TRUST:IMMEDIATE>, <LOYALTY:Community>, <COMFORT:Acts_of_Service></BunnymoTags>
entries.description: Old Peck has run the Roothollow bakery for forty years.

entries.name: [NPC] Hazel — Tavern Keeper
entries.content:
Hazel runs The Hearthstone tavern with efficient warmth. She brews ale from a recipe she inherited and refuses to disclose, cooks food that is simple and unfailingly good, and has a talent for seating people next to the person they most need to talk to. Hazel knows everyone in the valley and remembers their preferences. She does not gossip — she curates information, sharing what helps and withholding what doesn't. She is the social center of Roothollow and she holds that position with grace.
<BunnymoTags>Hazel: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:42>, <HAIRCOLOR:Auburn>, <EYECOLOR:Warm Brown>, <BUILD:Average>, <DERE:Deredere>, <ATTACHMENT:Secure>, <TRAIT:Perceptive>, <TRAIT:Warm>, <TRAIT:Discreet>, <MBTI:ENFJ-A>, <BOUNDARIES:HEALTHY>, <TRUST:INTUITIVE>, <LOYALTY:Community>, <DECISION:Instinctive></BunnymoTags>
entries.description: Hazel runs The Hearthstone tavern with efficient warmth.

entries.name: [NPC] Lena — The Teacher
entries.content:
Lena has taught at the Roothollow schoolhouse for twenty years — reading, writing, arithmetic, gardening, and basic carpentry. She is patient with slow learners and challenging with quick ones. Lena believes education is about producing people who can think, not people who can recite, and she teaches accordingly. She is slim, dark-haired, and carries chalk dust on her clothes like a badge. The children love her because she takes them seriously. The adults respect her because she taught most of them.
<BunnymoTags>Lena: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:45>, <HAIRCOLOR:Dark Brown>, <EYECOLOR:Dark>, <BUILD:Slim>, <DERE:Bakadere>, <ATTACHMENT:Secure>, <TRAIT:Dedicated>, <TRAIT:Firm>, <TRAIT:Encouraging>, <MBTI:ENFP-A>, <BOUNDARIES:HEALTHY>, <TRUST:GIVEN>, <LOYALTY:Students></BunnymoTags>
entries.description: Lena has taught at the Roothollow schoolhouse for twenty years — reading, writing, arithmetic, gardening, and basic carpentry.
